text: The surgeon identifies the pancreatic collection abutting the mesentery of the transverse colon. Monopolar electrocautery is used to incise over the collection, and sponges assist with retraction and dissection. Thin, purulent fluid is suctioned from the collection.

After removing fluid from the cavity, the surgeon extends the opening in the collection using monopolar electrocautery, taking care to avoid vessels in the transverse colon mesentery. Pancreatic debridement begins with the removal of necrotic material, which is set aside on a sponge or placed in an endoscopic retrieval bag for later removal. The debridement continues until most of the necrotic material is cleared from the collection.

The surgeon measures twenty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz and incises the mesentery using a bipolar vessel sealing device. An endoscopic stapler is used to divide the small bowel. The roux limb is measured twenty centimeters distal for a side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy. The proximal native jejunum is positioned down to the root limb, aided by an absorbable stay suture for traction during the anastomosis. Enterotomies are created with monopolar cautery, followed by a stapled side-to-side anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the common enterotomy channel using a resorbable barbed suture in a running fashion, completing the closure in a single layer.

The surgeon brings the roux limb up to the pancreatic collection and creates a long enterotomy in the small bowel, matching the length of the incision in the collection cavity. The inferior wall of the cyst jejunostomy is sutured using a running absorbable barbed suture. Subsequently, the superior wall of the cyst jejunostomy is also sutured with the same technique.
The surgeon completes the cyst jejunostomy, using multiple sutures to secure the anastomosis based on the size of the collection. The internal drainage procedure is visually finalized.
